author: bridge-bot
content:
<jorkleo> re: **@Jommi**
> if only layerzero used lightclients huh
What you need is a client that does local verification —> colibri stateless 
https://github.com/corpus-core/colibri-stateless

author: bridge-bot
content: <etan_status> the tricky part is getting colibri / nimbus verified proxy / helios into the smart contract that accepts the data

author: bridge-bot
content: <etan_status> ethereum data structures today are simply not very efficient to prove, and efforts to make them more efficient have been lower priority compared to scaling efforts

author: bridge-bot
content:
<stkux> re: **@etan_status**
> the tricky part is getting colibri / nimbus verified proxy / helios into the smart contract that accepts the data
What do you mean with that? Of course, there could be optimizations to make it more efficient. But proofing state, logs, eth_call, etc. together with efficient consensus proof is possible, as it is implemented in colibri.stateless (see https://corpuscore.tech)

author: bridge-bot
content: <etan_status> higher gas cost, compared to status quo

author: bridge-bot
content: <etan_status> "there could be optimizations to make it more efficient" -> due to this, essentially

author: bridge-bot
content:
<stkux> re: **@etan_status**
> higher gas cost, compared to status quo
What gas costs? This doesn't effect any cost on chain. Of course, generating the proofs is more effort on the RPC side (btw. we have different modi for that: 
1. local prover - data is read from RPC and the proofs are generated locally and more RPC requests (e.g., for eth_getProof, data from beacon chain for consensus proof) are needed. Also, there are restriction with historical data.
2. prover service -  the data is read from RPC and the proofs are provided by prover services (still fully trustless) - which is more performant and no restrictions with historical data
3. vRPC (verifiable RPC) - Data and proofs are provided by the RPC provider -> RPC becomes trustless.

So, yes, it is more data read from the RPC which might consume a little bit more request tokens. Also, the latency for generating the proofs will add to the RPC's latency, but this is in the milliseconds area...

author: bridge-bot
content: <etan_status> if the 1/1 signer itself is compromised, it can just be patched to.. ignore the proofs

author: bridge-bot
content:
<stkux> re: **@etan_status**
> if the 1/1 signer itself is compromised, it can just be patched to.. ignore the proofs
If the signer is compromised, it can ignore proofs - or could do anything else. 
What we solve with stateless clients is NOT that an app can't be compromised but that an app must not trust any data it receives from data providers. So, the app can't be tricked by manipulated data.

author: bridge-bot
content: <etan_status> ... unless the contract accepting the sig itself contains the LC verifier, which, today, is unnecessarily inefficient

author: bridge-bot
content: <etan_status> I agree with you, though, that colibri/helios/nimbus on the RPC client side always is better than not having it 🙂 would have at least caught that the local geth is compromised
